title: Kate Hawley on Costuming Guillermo del Toro's Frankenstein
text: Oscar-nominated costume designer Kate Hawley discusses her work on del Toro's adaptation of Mary Shelley's classic novel, emphasizing how garments blur the distinction between humanity and monstrosity. Hawley explains that color functions as narrative language throughout the film. The recurring blood-red motif from Claire Frankenstein's opening dress to the final scenes creates visual continuity representing death and the mother's influence on Victor's obsession. The character design draws inspiration from rock and roll iconography. Hawley notes that Oscar Isaac's extreme physicality informed the approach with irreverently worn and beautifully disheveled costumes that convey Victor's chaotic genius rather than refined precision. Hawley drew inspiration from Louis Comfort Tiffany's Favrile glass work and the Art Nouveau movement, creating rich, layered tones rather than bright saturation. This painterly approach maintains gothic atmosphere while incorporating vibrant colors. The Creature's silhouette deliberately questions whether he appears beast or human. Garments from the graveyard become memories of another man, with the costume's scale shifting as the character grows more powerful throughout the narrative.
